relationship_corrections.reason: Multiple audit entries can belong to one fraud requirement, not a one-to-one association. Many suspicious transactions can be linked to the same fraud detection rule.
A fraud detection requirement typically involves a single interface (the fraud detection system), not many. The multiplicity should be 1 for the interface.

A user owns a card via composition, but the cardinality should be one-to-one (each user has one card, each card belongs to one user). The arrow should be 'o--' (aggregation) since the card can exist without the user, but *-- implies many users share one card, which is incorrect.
Similarly, a user has one PIN, and a PIN belongs to one user. Use aggregation (o--) rather than composition with reversed multiplicity.

architecture_corrections.description: User inherits from Resource via association arrow --|>, but Resource represents a generic concept and User should be a specific Actor, not a Resource.

details.reason: User is a type of actor, not a resource.

architecture_corrections.description: FinancialDatabase is an interface but is used as a concrete class in composition with Account.

details.reason: It should be a port/interface, not a concrete class.

architecture_corrections.description: AuditEntry, AuditLog, AuditLogEntry likely represent the same concept; tasks #87 and #84 refer to audit logs.
Account should have a composition or aggregation relationship with Card and PIN to reflect card-and-PIN authentication.

architecture_suggestions.suggestion: Rename to BalanceAndLimitPort for consistency with hexagonal architecture and to avoid mixing database implementation with port.
Operation appears to be a catch-all for user actions; consider splitting into WithdrawalOperation, AuthenticationOperation, etc., for clarity.
Consider making Card and PIN value objects within the Account aggregate to simplify identity and lifecycle management.
Introduce an Account aggregate root with Transaction as an entity inside it to enforce invariants like balance checks and daily limits.
